text: The surgeon inserts a 12mm trocar supraumbilically to the left of the midline for the robotic camera, followed by four additional trocars in the left upper quadrant. The left lobe of the liver is retracted anteriorly using a Nathanson retractor, exposing the hiatus. A large parasophageal hernia is visible, with the entire stomach herniated into the posterior mediastinum and rotated, with the greater curvature located near the right pleura.

The surgeon gently reduces the herniated stomach and divides the short gastric vessels. The hernia sac is sectioned starting at its junction with the left pleura, extending towards the right side around the rim of the hiatus. The left crest is exposed, and the greater curvature of the stomach is freed. Dissection is extended into the posterior mediastinum, where a Penrose drain is placed.

The surgeon focuses on the right side of the esophagus, opening the pars flaccida and identifying the right crest. Using blunt dissection and a robotic vessel sealer device, the crest is separated from the esophagus, extending from anterior to posterior. The Penrose drain is used to encircle and retract the esophagus, facilitating dissection of the hernia sac from its mediastinal attachments, reducing it into the abdomen and leaving it at the gastroesophageal junction level.

The surgeon mobilizes the esophagus towards the left side, both anteriorly and posteriorly, to achieve adequate intra-abdominal length. The aorta is visible, curving into the left chest, with the esophagus oriented similarly due to the patient's scoliosis. Areolar attachments are dissected to straighten the esophagus and increase its length.

The surgeon begins closure of the diaphragmatic defect after complete mobilization of the esophagus and observation of the GE junction within the abdomen. Five interrupted O-silk sutures are used for primary closure, followed by reinforcement with a U-shaped Bio-A mesh secured to the right and left crura with two O-soaked sutures. The hernia sac is resected using a vessel sealer and removed from the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon performs a Nissen fundoplication over a 56 French bougie, using three 2-0 silk stitches to create the wrap. The most distal stitch secures the wrap to the esophagus. An upper endoscopy is conducted to confirm the procedure's success.
